target_question: Why do mutable default arguments in Python cause unexpected behavior?
description: Why using a mutable object like a list as a default argument causes it to persist across calls
initial_misconception: Default arguments are re-created fresh every time you call the function.
initial_student_statement: I have a function with def add_item(item, lst=[]). Every time I call it, the list keeps growing! But the default should be a new empty list each time, right?
correct_understanding: Default arguments in Python are evaluated once when the function is defined, not each time it's called. A mutable default like [] is created once and shared across all calls that don't provide that argument.
good_question_templates: When do you think Python creates that default empty list?
What if the default value was created only once — what would that mean?
Can you check if the list object is the same one across two calls using id()?
What's the difference between when a function is defined versus when it's called?
What would happen if the default was an immutable value like None instead?

confused: What do you mean 'when it's created'? The default is [] so it should just be a new list each time?
I don't understand — [] literally means 'make a new empty list,' doesn't it?
uncertain: Hmm, so the [] in the function signature... it's not like writing lst = [] inside the function?
Wait, are you saying the default list existed before I even called the function?
starting_to_see: Oh... so the list is created when Python reads the def statement, not when I call the function? So there's only one list object?
So every call that doesn't pass a list argument gets the same list? That's why it keeps growing!
almost_there: I see — the default is a single object that persists. That's why people use None as the default and create a new list inside the function!
So the fix is def add_item(item, lst=None): if lst is None: lst = [] — because None is immutable and we create a new list each call.
understood: Got it! Python evaluates default arguments once at function definition time. Mutable defaults like [] are shared across all calls, so they accumulate state. The fix is to use None and create the mutable inside the function body.
I understand now — it's about when the default is evaluated. Definition time, not call time. That's a fundamental Python behavior, not a bug.
